behaviors.description: The speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: The real failure boundary is STT diarization quality (LLM-DEP equivalent for speech model output): mocked transcripts hide mislabeled/missing speakers. Integration alone is insufficient because the core risk is model output correctness, not just local handoff. Workflow was considered, but the behavior text centers on STT output preservation; a single real-model step plus attribution assertions is enough to catch the target failure.
behaviors.test_description: Run real meeting audio through the production STT model and assert speaker labels are present/consistent in transcript segments, then verify attribution fields in the produced summary map to those labels. Fail on missing, collapsed, or swapped speaker identities.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real LLM extraction and attribution quality from transcript content; stubs will systematically overstate success (MOCK-HIDE). No OS/network artifact is the core failure. Workflow was considered, but this behavior can be validated from a fixed transcript against one real summarization step, so multiple agentic stages are not required by the behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Use a transcript with known attendee-task assignments, call the real summarization model, and check that each attendee with an assigned task has at least one action item attributed to them in structured output.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the "decisions" section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: This is output-quality placement/extraction from a real LLM; deterministic mocks cannot expose omissions or section misplacement. Integration is too low because module wiring can pass while model behavior fails. Workflow was considered, but the behavior text targets one summarization artifact (section placement), not a multi-step agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Provide transcripts containing explicit decision statements, invoke the real summarizer, and assert those decisions are present in the structured `decisions` field (and not only in free-text narrative fields).

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: Failure requires chained behavior across multiple agentic steps (per-segment transcription/summarization + merge coherence). Single-step Agentic misses cross-segment drift, duplication, or contradiction introduced during sequential merge (MULTI-STEP => Workflow). System concerns may exist, but the defining risk is multi-step agentic composition.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real >60-minute recording with known cross-segment references. Run the production split/process/merge pipeline end-to-end and verify: segment boundaries are respected, processing order is sequential, and final summary is coherent (no dropped or duplicated action items/decisions across segment joins).

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: This explicitly requires end-to-end execution across real infrastructure and multiple AI steps. Unit/Integration cannot catch deployment/runtime and orchestration failures; System alone is insufficient because success depends on sequential agentic stages, not just infra connectivity.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the production pipeline on a real meeting file from upload through delivered structured summary, with no manual retries or edits. Assert completion status, valid structured schema, and presence of core sections (action items, decisions, attendee attribution).
